title: Page Curve for Local-Operator Entanglement from Free Probability
abstract: The local-operator entanglement (LOE) measures the classical simulability of a Heisenberg operator and is conjectured to witness many-body chaos in locally interacting systems. Using tools from free probability, we analytically compute its value for Haar random dynamics for all Rényi indices. We find that it asymptotically reproduces the Page curve for random states in the case of traceless operators, with exponentially deviating corrections. In contrast to higher-order out-of-time ordered correlators, which depend on operator correlations via free cumulants, the leading-order LOE is independent of the initial operator. Guided by our Haar result, we therefore argue that the long-time value of the LOE entropies in chaotic systems will depend only on autocorrelation functions of the initial operator up to exponentially small corrections, suggesting that the higher-order structure of the full Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis is not necessary to describe it.
summary: This paper uses free probability to analytically derive the Page curve for Local-Operator Entanglement in chaotic systems. It demonstrates that the leading-order entanglement of operators is independent of the initial operator's higher-order correlations, depending only on two-point functions. This suggests that the long-time behavior of LOE is governed by standard ETH rather than the full ETH.
main_problem: Determining the Page curve for Local-Operator Entanglement (LOE) in chaotic systems and investigating whether its long-time behavior depends on higher-order correlations (full ETH) or only on two-point correlations (standard ETH).
main_result: The authors analytically show that the LOE asymptotically reproduces the Page curve for random states for traceless operators, and that the leading-order LOE is independent of the initial operator, depending only on its autocorrelation functions.
method: The study employs free probability theory, Weingarten calculus, and the Brauer algebra to compute Rényi entropies and purities in the large-dimension limit.
model_or_system: The system consists of many-body chaotic quantum systems undergoing Haar-random unitary dynamics, analyzed via a spatial bipartition of a D-dimensional Hilbert space.
key_observables: Local-Operator Entanglement (LOE), k-Rényi entanglement entropy, operator k-purity, and free cumulants.
important_parameters: Large-dimension limit (D >> 1), Rényi indices (k), subsystem size (NA), and the distinction between traceless and finite-trace operators.
main_assumptions: The results are primarily valid in the large-D asymptotic limit, assuming Haar-random dynamics and self-averaging of the entanglement properties.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the LOE Page curve for 8 qubits compared to the maximum possible entropy; Figure 2 illustrates the non-crossing partition lattice for k=4, showing the geometric structure of permutations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the LOE concept, applies free probability and Weingarten calculus to derive the Page curve, compares operator entanglement to state entanglement, and discusses implications for the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH).
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a rigorous link between operator scrambling, chaos, and the statistical properties of entanglement, suggesting that higher-order ETH structure is not required to describe LOE.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The LOE Page Curve for N = 8 qubits as a function of subsystem size NA [blue, circles], alongside the maximum possible entropy [red, dotted]. At the half-chain bipartition, the correction to maximum is given by the state Page correc- tion of 1/2 plus an exponentially small (in N) value dependent on the fourth free-cumulant of O, κ4(O). Inset: the differ- ence between the (annealed) average 2-Rényi entanglement entropies of states (in doubled Hilbert space) versus opera- tors, for NA = N ¯ A.

Figure 2. The non-crossing partition lattice for k = 4. Each node corresponds to a permutation, written below in stan- dard cyclic notation, while diagrammatically the isomorphic partition is displayed. We can identify the non-crossing par- tition subset of permutations corresponding to diagrams that do not cross; cf. the crossing partition depicted in the top right. Each line identifies unit distance according to the Cay- ley metric [Eq. (30)], while moving along a wire up the dia- gram corresponds to the (partial) ordering π ≤σ [Eq. (12)]. In purple [left] and green [right] are the geodesics σ ≤τγ and σ ≤τe.
